name: Guinea-Bissau (Republic of Guinea-Bissau / República da Guiné-Bissau)
summary: Guinea-Bissau is an Atlantic West African civil-law unitary republic whose family-law framework operates under the Civil Code (Código Civil) drawing on Portuguese civil-law substantive heritage (via 1973 independence retention), supplemented by Law 4/2011 on Domestic Violence and customary-law jurisdiction. Parental responsibility (responsabilidade parental) and child custody are governed by Civil Code arts. 1874-1920. The Supreme Court of Justice (Supremo Tribunal de Justiça) is the apex court for civil and criminal matters. Family-law matters are heard at first instance in the Court of Sector (Tribunal de Sector). Psychology profession is regulated through the Ministry of Health framework. Guinea-Bissau is silent on 'parental alienation' as a statutory label; courts operate substantively under the superior-interest-of-the-child standard. Guinea-Bissau is non-Hague Convention.

statutory_framework.title: Civil Code — Parental responsibility and custody
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal Civil Code drawing on Portuguese Civil Code 1966 substantive heritage. Arts. 1874-1920 govern responsabilidade parental and child custody.

statutory_framework.title: Law on Domestic Violence
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal Law on Domestic Violence affecting family-law proceedings.

apex_courts.name: Supreme Court of Justice (Supremo Tribunal de Justiça)
apex_courts.role: Apex court for civil and criminal matters.

professional_regulators.name: Ministry of Public Health, Guinea-Bissau
professional_regulators.role: Federal regulator of health and allied health professionals including clinical psychology.

anonymisation_convention: Bissau-Guinean family-court decisions are anonymised per Supreme Court practice using initials.

key_developments.title: Guinea-Bissau independence + Amílcar Cabral legacy
key_developments.description: Guinea-Bissau declared independence 24 September 1973, formally recognised by Portugal 10 September 1974 following Carnation Revolution. Substantively unique among Lusophone African states for unilateral independence-declaration prior to Portuguese-recognition. Amílcar Cabral (assassinated 20 January 1973) — among most distinctive African anti-colonial revolutionary intellectuals. Portuguese Civil Code 1966 substantive heritage substantively retained as foundational legal-system substrate.

key_developments.title: Coup d'état 1980 + post-Cabral political-institutional context
key_developments.description: Coup d'état 14 November 1980 ended Luís Cabral presidency (Amílcar Cabral's half-brother) — João Bernardo 'Nino' Vieira assumed presidency 1980-1999. Substantively distinctive recurring-coup-d'état political-instability pattern persisting through subsequent decades affecting family-law-implementation.

key_developments.title: Guinea-Bissau ratifies UN Convention on the Rights of the Child
key_developments.description: Guinea-Bissau ratified the UNCRC on 20 August 1990 — among the early African ratifications globally. Framing the family-law-reform trajectory toward best-interests-of-the-child substantive doctrine within Portuguese-civil-law-derivative framework.

key_developments.title: Multi-party transition + 1993 Constitution
key_developments.description: Multi-party transition 1991 — Constitution adopted 16 May 1984 (subsequently amended) establishing semi-presidential republic framework. Subsequent amendments 1991, 1993, 1996 modernised political-institutional framework affecting family-law-adjacent areas.

key_developments.title: Guinea-Bissau Civil War 1998-1999 + political-institutional disruption
key_developments.description: Guinea-Bissau Civil War 7 June 1998 – 7 May 1999 between government forces and military rebellion — substantially disrupted political-institutional framework. Subsequent recurring-coup-d'état pattern 2003, 2009, 2010, 2012 affecting governance stability.

key_developments.title: Family Code reform discussions + persistent customary-law context
key_developments.description: Family Code reform discussions through 2000s-2010s within Portuguese-civil-law-derivative framework. Substantive customary-law jurisdiction operating alongside formal civil-law framework — particularly affecting rural areas. Among the most distinctive customary-law-formal-law hybrid frameworks within Lusophone Africa.

key_developments.title: Law 4/2011 on Domestic Violence
key_developments.description: Federal Law on Domestic Violence enacted 6 July 2011 — establishing protection orders, mandatory-reporting obligations, multi-disciplinary response framework, and explicit recognition of psychological violence within the family unit. Substantively significant within Guinea-Bissau context given customary-law-civil-law-hybrid framework.

key_developments.title: Law on FGM Prohibition + child-protection framework
key_developments.description: Law on Female Genital Mutilation Prohibition enacted 2011 with subsequent implementation 2014+. Substantive child-protection framework expansion. Operates alongside Civil Code parental-responsibility framework within Portuguese-derivative-+-customary-law-hybrid framework.

key_developments.title: Coup attempt 2022 + Embaló presidency context
key_developments.description: Coup attempt 1 February 2022 — Umaro Sissoco Embaló presidency 2020-present substantively affected by recurring political-instability pattern. Persistent challenges to constitutional-democratic framework affecting family-law-implementation.

key_developments.title: ECOWAS engagement + UN-PBF framework + IMC institutional position
key_developments.description: Guinea-Bissau continuing ECOWAS engagement framework substantively significant West African institutional context. UN Peacebuilding Fund (UN-PBF) framework substantively significant for institutional capacity building. Instituto da Mulher e da Criança (IMC) institutional position addressing PA-related issues within Civil Code + Law 4/2011 framework. Substantively significant Lusophone West African institutional jurisprudential development.

key_developments.title: Supremo Tribunal de Justiça — superior-interest-of-the-child substantive register
key_developments.description: Supremo Tribunal de Justiça continues to develop superior-interest-of-the-child jurisprudence under Civil Code arts. 1874-1920 + Law 4/2011 DV + customary-law-hybrid framework in custody disputes without adopting the 'parental alienation' label as a doctrinal term. Substantive analysis within Portuguese-civil-law + customary-law hybrid framework operating within recurring-political-instability context.

structural_findings: Guinea-Bissau operates a Portuguese-civil-law family-law framework — places Guinea-Bissau in the Lusophone African cluster with Cabo Verde, Mozambique, Angola, São Tomé within the corpus.
Lusophone West African Atlantic position is structurally distinctive within West Africa — Guinea-Bissau is the only Lusophone state in West Africa within the corpus.
Non-Hague Convention status places Guinea-Bissau in the non-Hague West African cluster.
editorial_notes: Guinea-Bissau jurisdiction sidecar v1.1 — deepened 2026-06-08 from 2 to 10 key_developments with full Cabral-to-Embaló trajectory: 1973-Guinea-Bissau-independence-+-Amílcar-Cabral-legacy + 1980-Coup-d'état-post-Cabral + 1990-UNCRC-ratification + 1991-Multi-party-transition-+-Constitution + 1998-1999-Guinea-Bissau-Civil-War + 2008-Family-Code-reform-discussions-+-customary-law-context + 2011-Law-4-2011-Domestic-Violence + 2014-Law-on-FGM-Prohibition + 2022-Coup-attempt-Embaló-presidency-context + 2024-Supremo-Tribunal-de-Justiça-superior-interest-of-the-child.
Civil-law Lusophone West African Atlantic (Portuguese Civil Code 1966 + Law 4/2011 Domestic Violence + Law on FGM Prohibition 2011/2014 + customary-law hybrid + non-Hague Convention).

PA-recognition: silent statutory + no-apex-position + silent regulator — substantive superior-interest-of-the-child analysis under Civil Code arts. 1874-1920 + customary-law-hybrid framework without doctrinal 'parental alienation' label.

Joins West-African + Lusophone-Africa + civil-law + only-Lusophone-West-Africa-distinctive + Amílcar-Cabral-revolutionary-legacy + Portuguese-Civil-Code-1966-derivative-substrate + customary-law-civil-law-hybrid-distinctive + recurring-coup-d'état-political-instability-pattern-1980-2003-2009-2010-2012-2022 + Guinea-Bissau-Civil-War-1998-1999 + non-Hague-Convention clusters within the corpus.
